behaviors.description: Log entries with the same trace ID are grouped together regardless of which microservice emitted them
behaviors.justification: This is pure in-process grouping logic operating on structured data. No I/O, no external dependencies, no performance concerns. A mock of the grouping function couldn't hide failures — either the logic groups correctly or it doesn't. Test data can be constructed as dictionaries or model instances.
behaviors.test_description: Create log entries with multiple trace IDs from different microservices (e.g., 'auth-service', 'api-gateway', 'db-service'). Group them by trace ID. Verify that all entries with the same trace ID are in the same group, different trace IDs are in separate groups, and no entries are lost or duplicated.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: The test only requires in-process data structures and basic Python logic, no integration or I/O

behaviors.description: The timeline view orders entries by timestamp, correctly handling clock skew of up to 500ms between services
behaviors.justification: This is pure sorting/comparison logic. Clock skew doesn't require real distributed clocks — we can simulate it by constructing test timestamps that are out of order. The sorting algorithm either handles out-of-order input correctly or doesn't. No real infrastructure needed.
behaviors.test_description: Create log entries with timestamps that are intentionally out of order to simulate clock skew between services (e.g., entries from 'service-a' at t=1000, t=1100; entries from 'service-b' at t=1050, t=1020). Include some entries with 500ms skew. Sort/order them. Verify the final timeline is in correct chronological order and the skew is handled without corruption.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Simulated clock skew in test data is sufficient; no need for real clock drift between systems

behaviors.description: A latency spike (p99 above 3x the baseline) is detected and flagged in the anomaly report
behaviors.justification: This is pure statistical computation — calculating percentiles and comparing ratios. Latency values can be simulated with test data; no need for real request execution. The algorithm either detects the spike or it doesn't. A mock of the percentile function might hide failures, but testing with real percentile logic on constructed data is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Create two sets of request latencies: (1) baseline with values like [80ms, 85ms, 90ms, ..., 150ms] yielding p99≈145ms, and (2) spike set with the same baseline plus outliers [80ms, 90ms, ..., 500ms, 400ms] yielding p99>435ms. Feed both to the detection algorithm. Verify the baseline is not flagged and the spike set is flagged. Also test that gradual increases just under 3x don't trigger false positives.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Test data with constructed latencies is sufficient; no real request execution required

behaviors.description: Malformed JSON log entries are skipped without crashing the pipeline, and the count of skipped entries is reported
behaviors.justification: Pure mocking of the JSON parser would hide real failures — the tool must actually attempt to parse real malformed JSON to verify error handling. This requires integration of the JSON parser with the pipeline's error-handling logic. We need actual parsing to happen (not stubbed), but can use local test files or in-memory strings.
behaviors.test_description: Create a mix of valid and malformed JSON log entries as test data: valid entries (properly formatted JSON objects), missing closing braces ('{ "trace_id": "123"'), invalid escape sequences ('{ "msg": "test\x99" }'), truncated entries ('{ "trace_id"'), null bytes, etc. Process this input through the pipeline. Verify: (1) valid entries are parsed and added to the timeline, (2) malformed entries are skipped without raising exceptions, (3) the skipped entry count is reported correctly, and (4) the pipeline continues processing subsequent valid entries.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Requires real JSON parsing to fail and be caught; mocking would mask this. Local test data is sufficient (no distributed setup needed)

behaviors.description: The tool handles a burst of 10,000 log entries per second without dropping entries or falling behind
behaviors.justification: Load/throughput testing inherently depends on real I/O and resource contention characteristics. A pure in-process mock would artificially speed up processing and hide latency/backpressure issues. The tool likely reads from files or network; real I/O patterns are needed to reveal bottlenecks. This requires measuring actual elapsed time under realistic load, not just running logic fast.
behaviors.test_description: Generate or prepare 10,000+ test log entries (in a file, stream, or via subprocess feed). Deliver them to the tool as a burst (all within 1-2 seconds, simulating a traffic spike). Measure: (1) end-to-end processing time, (2) number of entries successfully processed vs. received, (3) memory usage during burst, (4) any dropped or re-queued entries. Verify no entries are lost and processing completes without timeout or resource exhaustion. Repeat with 50,000 and 100,000 entries to verify scaling.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Requires real I/O patterns (files, streaming, or subprocess) and timing measurements to avoid mock-hiding dropped entries or latency issues
